coredata.dc:description: The sustainable utilization of polymers depends on efficient recycling and the ability to retain their critical physical properties for further processing. In this study, high-density polyethylene (HDPE) nanocomposite properties were enhanced by the integration of carbon dots (CDs), in terms of processability and optical traceability during recycling. HDPE composites with varying CDs loadings were prepared to assess their effects on optical and mechanical properties over three consecutive recycling cycles. The composite containing 0.5 wt% CDs demonstrated a 17% increase in tensile strength after recycling, with a maximum strain of 11%, significantly outperforming the neat HDPE while preserving its crystalline structure. Additionally, incorporating 0.1 wt% CDs reduced the wear rate by up to 98%, highlighting a substantial improvement in durability. Improved processability of the recycled material was confirmed by producing 3D-printed specimens at each CDs concentration. Notably, composites containing 0.1 wt% CDs exhibited excellent printability even after three recycling cycles. CDs have also been utilized as luminescence tracers. This study revealed that the quenching of the blue phosphorescence associated to the carbonyl groups of the polymer backbone was highly dependent on the CDs content. Importantly, nanocomposites with 0.1 wt% CDs exhibited progressive luminescence changes corresponding to the number of recycling cycles, enabling quick and reliable traceability and sorting using standard mobile phone cameras. These findings are highly promising, paving the way for rapid, automated, and scalable HDPE recycling. This innovation offers significant potential for advancing the circular economy of HDPE and enhancing the sustainability of polymer materials.

(A) − Crystallinity degree of the HDPE composites for different CDs concentrations and recycling cycles; (B) − Hardness modulus comparison for different CDs concentrations and recycling cycles; (C) − Tensile strength comparison for different CDs concentrations and recycling cycles; (D) – Specific wear rate of tested materials. *The specific wear rate of HDPEV2 is 7.33 ± 1.55 × 10-7. All numerical values can be seen in Table S4.

SEM images of pins with a 20× (left), 100× (middle) and 400× (right) magnification. (A) HDPE05V1, (B) HDPE05V3. Red circles highlight accumulated wear debris and blue arrows indicate abrasive lines. The scale bars in the left column represent 1 mm, the ones in the middle column 200 µm, the ones in the right column 50 µm. SEM images of countersurfaces at 50 × magnification. (C) HDPE1V1, (D) HDPE05V3. White arrows on the side indicate the sliding direction.

(A) − PL and PLE spectra of HDPE. The PL spectrum was obtained with 325 nm excitation (450 W Xe lamp) and the PLE was monitored at 411 nm; (B) − Spectra of the neat polymer for each recycling cycle under 254 nm excitation; (C) − Luminescent intensity of each composite at the different recycling stages; (D) − Spectra of the 0.1 wt% sample for each recycling step under 254 nm excitation.

(A) − Photograph of the printed samples when excited at 254 nm; (B) Schematic representation of the photograph acquisition of the specimens by smartphone under UV light irradiation. (C) Intensity of each sample after the computational calculation from the cellphone photograph recorded at an excitation at 254 nm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sustainable utilization of polymers depends on efficient recycling and the ability to retain their critical physical properties for further processing. In this study, high-density polyethylene (HDPE) nanocomposite properties were enhanced by the integration of carbon dots (CDs), in terms of processability and optical traceability during recycling. HDPE composites with varying CDs loadings were prepared to assess their effects on optical and mechanical properties over three consecutive recycling cycles. The composite containing 0.5 wt% CDs demonstrated a 17% increase in tensile strength after recycling, with a maximum strain of 11%, significantly outperforming the neat HDPE while preserving its crystalline structure. Additionally, incorporating 0.1 wt% CDs reduced the wear rate by up to 98%, highlighting a substantial improvement in durability. Improved processability of the recycled material was confirmed by producing 3D-printed specimens at each CDs concentration. Notably, composites containing 0.1 wt% CDs exhibited excellent printability even after three recycling cycles. CDs have also been utilized as luminescence tracers. This study revealed that the quenching of the blue phosphorescence associated to the carbonyl groups of the polymer backbone was highly dependent on the CDs content. Importantly, nanocomposites with 0.1 wt% CDs exhibited progressive luminescence changes corresponding to the number of recycling cycles, enabling quick and reliable traceability and sorting using standard mobile phone cameras. These findings are highly promising, paving the way for rapid, automated, and scalable HDPE recycling. This innovation offers significant potential for advancing the circular economy of HDPE and enhancing the sustainability of polymer materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over recent decades, the widespread use of plastics has risen sharply due to population growth and technological advancements, with only 7% of plastics recycled annually [1]. Plastic production has increased more than expected because of its ability to replace metal, wood, paper, and glass in a variety of engineering applications and is estimated to be around 1.1 billion tons by 2050 [1–3]. Plastics like high-density polyethylene (HDPE) have replaced traditional materials in various applications due to their low cost and versatility, but their limited biodegradability contributes to environmental contamination. Recycling, particularly mechanical recycling, offers a sustainable solution to plastic waste, though the properties of recycled materials, such as tensile strength and hardness, can degrade over multiple cycles [4]. HDPE represents 15–25% of all plastic waste globally. Similar to other thermoplastics, HDPE can be recycled to be used again [5]. Nevertheless, it is important to evaluate whether the performance of recycled materials is affected by each recycling cycle, particularly in terms of properties such as the tensile strength, yield strength, and hardness modulus [6]. For instance, Oblak et al. [7] processed HDPE through 100 extrusion cycles, and the characterization revealed that the hardness of the polymer was almost unaffected after 10 extrusion cycles; after 100 cycles, the reduction was approximately 20% when compared with its initial value. During the recycling process, the material is exposed to thermo-mechanical and thermo-oxidative conditions, which can result in chain scission and other reactions such as branching and crosslinking [8]. Several studies in recent years have addressed the applicability of recycled HDPE for additive manufacturing [9–14]. For example, Bedi et al. [14] performed a study investigating the use of recycled HDPE reinforced with aluminum oxide (Al2O3) for rapid tooling applications. The objective of this study was to assess their performance by evaluating their tool life, hardness, porosity, and other relevant mechanical properties. Thermoplastic granules were obtained from recycled household waste mechanically processed and blended with reinforcements in a commercial twin-screw extruder (TSE). Filaments were prepared and mechanically tested using a universal tensile tester. Subsequently, they were used to print 3D parts to verify their printing capability [14]. In a similar study, also using Al2O3, Singh et al. [10] prepared polymer blends of HDPE and ceramic materials in different proportions to investigate their thermal and mechanical properties. The proportions used were 10, 20, and 30% (weight percentage), revealing a higher melt flow index (MFI) value across all samples, which is a good indicator of injection molding. In terms of the mechanical properties of the composite, it was concluded that the best results were obtained for a 10% reinforcement material, making it a suitable candidate for room temperature (RT) applications [10].

Over the years, relevant research has been conducted on reinforcement materials to create polymer composites with enhanced mechanical properties. Among these, one of the most widely used is carbon-based nanoparticles, with several examples of 1D and 2D carbon structures, namely carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene [15–21]. For example, Hu et al. [22] fabricated CNTs-based carbon-fiber-reinforced HDPE composites with remarkable mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties. The results showed that the tensile and flexural strengths of the composites were improved by 70% and 40%, respectively, when compared to those of neat HDPE with 25% carbon fiber content. This result was further improved with the use of CNTs-coated carbon fibers, with a hardness increase of 22%. Considering the extensive research conducted in the field of CNTs and other carbon-based nanoparticles, it is reasonable to assume that consumer goods will start to enter the market. However, there are few studies regarding the use of zero-dimensional (0-D) nanoparticles, more specifically carbon dots (CDs), as reinforcement agents for polymeric materials. CDs are tunable luminescent nanomaterials with a size below 10 nm and a quasi-spherical morphology composed of an sp2/sp3 carbon core, with low toxicity, low production costs, and chemical stability, and have been proposed in several research fields owing to their optical and mechanical performance, among others [23]. Key factors such as the choice of carbon precursor, synthesis method, and chemical environment can influence the photoluminescent (PL) behavior of CDs. However, a consensus on this mechanism within the scientific community has not yet been reached [24]. For example, Sreenath et al. [25] studied the use of CDs as a reinforcing filler in carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene (XNBR), obtaining a significant improvement of the optical and physico-mechanical properties. They verified a constant increase in tensile strength with the increase in CDs concentration, up to a 215% increase for a 2 phr (parts per hundred ml) concentration. However, to the best of our knowledge, HDPE:CDs nanocomposites have not been previously studied as mechanical reinforcement agents. In this study, CDs were incorporated into HDPE to fabricate novel polymeric composites with improved mechanical and optical properties. In addition, the recyclability of different composites was studied to understand whether the percentage of CDs can affect the mechanical and tribological properties over multiple recycling cycles. The applications of HDPE have significantly expanded for commercial use owing to their suitability for additive manufacturing. Through 3D printing, a wide variety of products can be fabricated from diverse materials, with minimal supply chain management. Engineering components with intricate and complex geometries can be produced with reduced waste and lead times [26]. However, the printability of HDPE declines after multiple recycling cycles owing to mechanical and thermal degradation, which significantly affects its molecular structure and rheological properties [7,27]. In this study, we investigated the reinforcement of HDPE with a small fraction of CDs to extend its processability after several recycling cycles for 3D printing, following experiment planning described on the diagram in Supplementary information (Fig. S1). Additionally, we demonstrate that incorporating CDs into HDPE enables optical traceability of recycling cycles using a simple smartphone camera. These findings can significantly contribute to more sustainable industrial applications of HDPE by improving processability and facilitating large-scale recycling through the implementation of optical-based technologies.

The reagents used in the preparation of the HDPE:CDs composites, were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. The HDPE pellets, sourced from Braskem, have an average density of 960 kg/m3, a mass flow index of 7.3 g/10 min, a Young’s modulus ranging from 0.4 to 0.8 GPa, and a yield strength ranging from 17.9 to 29 MPa. CDs were purchased from Ray Techniques Ltd, with an average grain size between 3.5 and 6.0 nm, ash residue less than 0.1% (wt.), hydrophilic, and lyophilic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and processing of the HDPE:CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The composite material was obtained using a Brabender EC mixer at four different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.5, and 1%). The materials were processed at 180 °C for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture of HDPE and CDs, which was then ready to produce testing specimens, as illustrated in Fig. S2. The first batch is named “V1” and after being recycled one time and two times, it changes to V2 and V3, respectively, as depicted in Table S1. Further details of the processing of the HDPE:CDs composites are described in the Supplementary information in Section 1.1 of the experimental details. For the printing process of the HDPE:CDs composites, a fused filament fabrication 3D printer (Ender 3) was used with a printing volume of 220 × 220 × 250 mm3, a 0.4 mm nozzle, a maximum layer resolution of 0.1 mm, and a print precision of ± 0.1 mm. The sample was printed at 240 °C with the bed heated to 110 °C, using a layer height of 300 µm. The printed sample measured 35 × 38 mm2 and had a total height of 1 mm.

The RT Raman spectra of the used CDs were collected on a Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer equipped with a 600 gr/mm grating under the incidence of a 442 nm (∼ 2.8 eV) line from a continuous wave He-Cd laser (Kimmon IK Series) to evaluate their structural and morphological characteristics.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the CDs were obtained using a Jeol 2200FS operating at 200 kV in bright-field imaging mode and analyzed using Gatan Digital Micrograph software. Particle size was assessed using ImageJ 1.53f51 software. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, NanoScope IV SPM Controller, Veeco) was performed in tapping mode to compare with existing literature reports. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the HDPE:CDs composites were performed using a Rigaku Lx57B XRD instrument with a tube voltage of 40 kV and a tube current of 30 mA. The degree range selection was 5–65° with a slit width of 0.33°, step of 0.02°, and speed of 3°/min.

Additionally, crystallinity was assessed by Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) using a TA Instruments Discovery DSC 250 equipment with a temperature range of −180 to 725 °C, a temperature precision of ± 0.005 °C, and an enthalpy precision of ± 0.04%. Samples weighing approximately 5–10 mg were placed in an aluminum pan and heated between RT and 200 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere maintained at a flow rate of 20 ml/min. The composite samples were then heated to 200 °C for the first scan and cooled to RT at the same rate, aiming to study the changes produced in the polymer’s crystalline structure.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on all samples using a Metter Toledo TGA/DSC 3+ instrument with a temperature ramp of 25–800 °C, heating rate of 10 °C/min, and 80 ml/min nitrogen gas flow. Mechanical characterization was performed using five test bodies on a Shimadzu AGS-X-10kN, rated at 10 kN using a speed of 1 mm/min until 0.3% strain and after 50 mm/min until rupture. Additionally, nanoindentation was performed using a CSM Instruments nanoindenter with a Berkovic diamond tip by applying a force measurement ranging between 0.2 and 150 mN for a total of nine indentations per sample.

Tribological tests were performed using a Cameron-Plint TE77 reciprocating tribometer under dry conditions at RT. The material was cut into square 4 × 4 mm2 pin samples that were sliding on a stainless-steel counter surface (CS) with a roughness value of 0.25 μm Ra. Prior to the sample testing, the pins were ultrasonically cleaned in an ethanol bath. Six pin-on-plate tests were performed on each CS in a random order of tested pins per recycled batch. The data required for the calculation of the volumetric wear loss were obtained during the test using a linear variable differential transformer (LVDT). The testing setup is shown in Fig. S3, and the testing parameters are listed in Table S2. A JCM-6000 tabletop SEM (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to analyze the countersurfaces and pin surfaces of the tested specimens. Prior to imaging, the pins were sputtered with a 12 nm layer of platinum.

All samples and powders were assessed by RT photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) conducted in a Fluorolog-3 Horiba Scientific setup with a double additive grating Gemini 180 monochromator (1200 gr/mm and 2 × 180 mm) for excitation and a triple-grating iHR550 spectrometer for emission (1200 gr/mm and 550 mm). This system was equipped with a 450 W Xe lamp as the excitation source.

The structural and morphological properties of the CDs were then characterized. FTIR analysis revealed an absorption peak related to − OH groups at 3336 cm−1 and a characteristic aromatic CH stretching vibration at 2924 cm−1 (Fig. S4). A vibration mode for CH3 and CH2 was observed at 2853 cm−1, along with a CO/COOH vibrational absorption band at 1705 cm−1. Additionally, bands characteristic of CC bonds appeared at 1654 cm−1, a bending vibration of C-O/C-N band appear at 1436 cm−1, while a COC stretching vibration was detected at 1378 cm−1. Furthermore, a COOH and a C-O stretching vibration were observed at 1257 and 1114 cm−1, respectively [28,29]. The Raman spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 1 A. The spectrum is characterized by typical defect-activated D (ca. 1340 cm−1) and G (ca. 1580 cm−1) bands, indicating sp3 and sp2 carbon contributions, respectively, signifying that the material is graphitic [30]. The Raman modes overlap with a wide contribution from the additional scattering mechanisms and/or PL signals. CDs usually possess absorption bands in the 200–300 nm region, owing to the π–π * transition of the CC bonds and sp2 networks. In addition, in some cases, absorption bands between 250 and 400 nm were observed and ascribed to the n-π* transition of the CO or CN bonds [31–33]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the CDs exhibited a very weak PL intensity band at RT, which was independent of the excitation wavelength (260 nm and 350 nm). The observed luminescence spectrum was broad, covering the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared regions, with a maximum in the orange spectral region at approximately 550 nm. Typically, CDs consist of a carbon core and surface domains, the latter of which contains abundant functional groups responsible for the emission of CDs, as previously reported [23,34,35].

Moreover, the CDs were crystalline, showing characteristic lattice fringes with an in-plane interplanar distance of 2.03 Å, compatible with graphitic carbon in terms of interplanar distance measurements (Fig. 1C). By using bright-field HR-TEM imaging, CDs evidence a spherical-shaped morphology with an average particle diameter of 5.3 nm and a standard deviation of 2.3 nm, exhibiting some dispersion, as indicated by the respective histogram (Fig. 1D). The latter is in good agreement with the description provided by the commercial supplier. The AFM analysis showed that the CDs are not spherical particles, showing a maximum height of 1.78 nm and an average lateral size of approximately 13 nm (Fig. 1E). A significant discrepancy between the size distribution obtained by TEM and AFM analysis was observed, which can be understood by considering that AFM, being an indirect measurement, should have factored in the error associated with the cantilever tip dimensions, particularly in lateral measurements.

Thermal polymer processing has a significant impact on the degradation of HDPE. Extrusion speed, time, temperature, and oxygen content are some examples of factors that can lead to degradation [36]. Multiple experimental techniques, including XRD and DSC, can be used to macroscopically assess the extent of such degradation. The XRD patterns of the neat polymer and HDPE:CDs composites shown in Fig. S5A, exhibit well-defined diffraction maxima corresponding to the (110), (200), (210), and (011) diffraction planes located at 21.6°, 24.0°, 30.0°, and 36.0°, respectively, following the expected patterns for crystalline HDPE with an orthorhombic unit cell [37]. In addition, a small fraction of the amorphous phase was observed at ca. 19.5°. After the recycling cycles, the diffraction maxima were slightly shifted to higher angles, indicating a decrease in the interlayer distance [38]. As shown in Fig. S5B, the recycling cycles seem to have a noticeable effect on the diffraction maxima intensity owing to polymer degradation [38]. In the case of the HDPE:CDs composites, the same HDPE XRD patterns were measured, showing that neither changes in the polymer structure were observed nor that new crystalline phases were formed, as also identified in other HDPE composites [39]. A comparison between the HDPE and composite samples revealed that the reduction in the degree of crystallinity was more prominent for the HDPE samples (88%) than for the composites (61%), as depicted in Fig. S5. This is a good indicator of the role of CDs in the structure, contributing to the thermal stability, improving the interfacial bonding, and reducing the mobility of the polymer chains [6,10]. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S5B, the recycling of the neat polymer seems to slightly shift the position of the main peaks (110) and (200). For the remaining samples, this trend was also verified with a visible shift for the main peaks after the first recycling cycle of 0.6° (Fig. S5C–E), but no effect was observed with increasing CDs concentration up to 1%. This confirmed the hypothesis that the addition of CDs (at the concentrations used in this work) did not affect the original polymer crystalline structure.

On the other hand, the DSC analysis of the recycled samples with or without CDs was carried out to evaluate the crystalline nature of the samples’ structure. As shown in Fig. 2 A, there were no significant changes in the crystallinity of the samples with increasing CDs concentration. A slight increase was observed in some samples with increasing CDs content. Well-dispersed CDs with strong interfacial adhesion can improve crystallinity or act as nucleating agents for HDPE, indirectly enhancing mechanical properties, as previously reported by Vashistha et al. [40,41]. The same trend was observed for the remaining samples; there were no significant changes in the degree of crystallinity with increasing CDs content, but crystallinity consistently decreased with each recycling cycle (Fig. 2A). It is important to note that the crystallinity of the composite samples was calculated considering both the polymer and CDs contents at different concentrations (w/w). Additionally, while analyzing the second heating and first cooling thermographs (Fig. S6) for the neat polymer and multi-processed composite samples, no significant changes were observed, and the shapes of the peaks remained unchanged for all the samples. There was only a slight shift to higher temperatures (in the 1st cooling) with each recycling cycle, indicating that the crystallization rate increased, resulting in a rapid crystallization period [42]. This behavior is usually attributed to thermomechanically induced degradation, which leads to the cross-linking of the recycled composites and the formation of smaller crystals. However, this trend was less pronounced for the sample with 1% CDs content, indicating that the presence of CDs prevented further degradation and crosslinking of the polymer chains. Additionally, TGA was performed on all samples, as the thermal degradation process is a significant issue in the recycling process, which is an important tool for establishing the degradation behavior of the polymer [43]. From the results obtained from the TGA analysis (Table S3), it was possible to clearly identify an increase in the onset temperature with an increase in the CDs content. This trend was also verified for the maximum temperature obtained before complete degradation. The process of thermal degradation of HDPE involves random chain scission followed by the formation of alkyl and alkyl peroxyl radicals [43]. With the addition of CDs, it was possible to delay the thermal oxidation of the composites, mainly because of the high thermal conductivity of the CDs, which allowed the heat generated by the test to dissipate. This trend was also observed in the thermographs displayed in Fig. S7, where neat HDPE maintained a constant weight until a temperature of 180 °C, after which degradation began. For the nanocomposite samples, the weight was maintained at 400 °C, which delayed thermal degradation of the sample. This behavior was also verified for the recycled samples, as illustrated in Fig. S7, reinforcing the belief that the presence of CDs protects the polymer matrix from early degradation, consisting of C-O bond formation and immediate decomposition to form CO and CO2. Another valid hypothesis is that the CDs are well dispersed into the polymer and thus act as free radical scavengers, avoiding the formation of the radicals responsible for thermal degradation, thus resulting in the observed higher composite degradation temperature [44]. In summary, the presence of CDs contributed positively to minimize polymer degradation, maintaining their properties unaffected. Thus, from an industrial perspective, it is possible to recover nanocomposites by mechanical recycling multiple times. These results also demonstrate the remarkable protective effect of the CDs against thermo-oxidative degradation.

Mechanical characterization of neat HDPE and HDPE:CDs composites was performed for each recycling cycle to evaluate mechanical properties such as tensile strength, yield strength, elongation, and hardness modulus, among other important parameters. Fig. 2B shows the results corresponding to the hardness modulus (HIT), in which HDPE and the first batch of samples with different CDs concentrations are displayed. For the composite with 0.1 wt% CDs, the HIT values obtained were 91.57, 66.49 and 62.65 MPa for the HDPE01CDs, HDPE01CDsV2 and HDPE01CDsV3, respectively. As the slight decrease observed was within the error bar, it is suggested that no significant differences in the HIT parameters were detected for different recycling cycles. However, for the sample with 0.5 wt% CDs concentration, an increase of up to 30% was observed in the HIT when compared with the virgin sample and even with the other studied concentrations. Moreover, by analyzing the influence of the recycling steps on the HIT parameter, the results are displayed in Fig. 2B, which shows that even though a decrease and increase in behaviors were calculated, it is very difficult to conclude that the hardness was improved. However, it is important to note that it remained similar, even after two recycling cycles.

Under short-term loads, polyethylene is usually characterized by a ductile elongation, with failure in that zone of the test sample [45], which was verified during the mechanical testing of the produced samples for the neat polymer and composite materials, even after undergoing two recycling cycles. In Fig. 2C it can be observed that the tensile strength of the composite materials slightly increased for all composite samples, and a best result of 26.19 MPa was obtained for the HDPE05CDs sample with a 9.5% increase. After the first recycling cycle, this trend was not verified; the tensile strength was very similar, except for HDPE05CDsV2, which increased by 17% without a significant reduction in elongation. For the second recycling cycle, the parameters exhibited very similar behavior without any significant changes. This observed tendency for the 0.5% CDs sample was also verified for other important parameters, such as the strain at the maximum load, which was calculated to be 11% for all recycling cycles, maintaining a similar behavior even after being recycled up to two times. This is in agreement with the findings of Svensson et al. [46], who studied the repeated processing of HDPE reinforced with CNTs, in which the nanocomposite was almost unaffected even after 200 min of processing. On the other hand, neat HDPE was stable up to 10 cycles of extrusion, with only a minor increase in tensile strength, possibly indicating a better alignment of the polymer chains as a function of increased chain mobility during processing [7]. This increase in tensile strength can also be attributed to thermo-mechanical degradation and chain scission; however, it has been demonstrated by Kadbi et al. [47] that in the case of low-density polyethylene (LDPE), after being broken down, the tensile strength can increase as it begins to crosslink. Therefore, as the CDs content increased, the overall crystallinity was maintained or even increased in the recycled samples, thereby increasing the tensile strength and hardness modulus. However, for HDPE1CDs, a modest increase was observed as the CDs content acted as a functional filler within the HDPE matrix rather than as an impurity owing to agglomeration. The neat polymeric samples exhibited a decreasing trend, corresponding to the worst-performing sample. The increase in tensile and yield strengths was not as significant as for composites with the addition of CNTs; however, our focus was to increase the material reprocessability and therefore decrease the observed thermal degradation occurring in the neat polymer. These results were expected because the recycling process, as established previously, degrades the polymer, leading to a brittle stage (chain scission) with increased hardness, decreased elongation, and early rupture. Following this observation, the next step was to evaluate the influence of each recycling step on the samples with 0.5 wt% CDs concentration. Fig. 2C displays the comparison for each recycling cycle for this specific composite, revealing a 17.6% increase in the recycled sample HDPE05CDsV2 compared to the neat polymer. The second recycled cycle (HDPE05CDsV3) showed a similar value for this parameter, which is in good agreement with the results obtained from the structural characterization. The degree of crystallinity of a sample has a significant influence on the physical and mechanical properties of the polymer [45]. However, even though the results for the degree of crystallinity are similar, the mechanical characterization revealed differences in the performance of the samples, which can be attributed to the presence of the CDs, contributing to the formation of long chains within the polymer, increasing the number of tie molecules, and therefore the tensile and yield strengths [48].

Tribological characterization was performed by analyzing the coefficient of friction (COF) and specific wear rate. As shown in Fig. S8, the initial results indicate that the COF slightly increased throughout the recycling cycle. The values range from 0.145 for neat HDPE and HDPEV2 to 0.154 for HDPEV3. The values of HDPE01CDs, HDPE01CDsV2, and HDPE01CDsV3 were 0.148, 0.154, and 0.162, respectively. The only case where an 8% reduction in the COF was observed was the case of 0.5 wt% added CDs content. Moreover, the addition of CDs did not significantly affect the COF. The performance of COF is mostly attributed to the interplay between the mechanical properties and crystalline phases within the composite. The resulting values were similar to those of the HIT presented in Fig. 2D. Higher HIT values exhibit lower COF, and vice versa. Karrupiah et al. [49] stated that friction is dependent on the polymers degree of crystallinity. Crystalline phases in the polymer are harder than their amorphous phases, which means that mechanical properties, such as hardness, are also dependent on the degree of crystallinity. In this case, the CD content increased the mechanical properties and slightly decreased COF.

The specific wear rate of the tested specimens indicated a significant loss in tribological properties throughout the recycling phase of the neat HDPE sample, as shown in Fig. 2D. The specific wear rate increased from 1.32 × 10-6 mm3/Nm for the neat HDPEV1, to 7.33 × 10-7 mm3/Nm in the case of HDPEV2 and to 7.46 × 10-6 mm3/Nm for HDPEV3. This increase was due to the loss of thermal and mechanical properties after recycling. An important observation is perceived in the cases with added CDs, where the specific wear rate was not significantly affected by the recycling phase. It remained around 1.44 × 10-6 mm3/Nm except for the case of HDPE05V3, where an increase from 1.25 × 10-6 (HDPE05V1) to 4.13 × 10-6 mm3/Nm was observed. The addition of CDs even at 0.1 wt%, substantially reduced the wear rate of the composite. Different additives and their amounts have previously been studied by Pelto et al. [50], where the addition of nano-additives improved the specific wear rate of HDPE composites with added halloysite nanotubes, fumed silica, titanium nitride, or graphene oxide at filler loadings of 0.5 and 1 wt%. The change was correlated to the mechanical properties rather than the degree of crystallinity, which was also the case for the composite in this work. SEM analysis of the sample pins after the tribo-test showed the presence of adhered particles (Fig. 3 ). However, abrasion caused by the roughness of the metal countersurface was a more prominent wear mechanism. This mechanism was more commonly observed in the case of composites with higher HIT, as shown in Fig. 3A and B, between HDPE05V1 and HDPE05V3. Because CDs are 0D nanomaterial, it is unlikely that this additive causes abrasive wear on the pins [51].

SEM images of the countersurfaces showed no transfer film deposition, except in the case of HDPE05V3. In Fig. 3, this composite is shown with HDPE1V1 (Fig. 3C) for comparison with a typical wear track after the test. In the case of HDPE05V3 (Fig. 3D), adhered patches of transfer film were observed on the countersurface, indicating the possible initiation of transfer film formation.

Usually, optically active defects in polymer materials are related to i) isolated chromophores located in the chain, side-chain, or end-chain groups and/or ii) chromophores present in the repeat units that form the backbone structure of the polymer. In addition, polymers are known to contain additives such as antioxidants, which are usually conjugated structures and can act as optically active defects [58]. In the case of the investigated polymer, which does not contain chromophores in the repeat unit of the macromolecule, chromophores are known to be unsaturated species or additives that are strongly or weakly bonded to the chain, respectively [53–58]. In particular, it can be seen in Fig. 4A that, with 325 nm excitation, a low-intensity emission maximum at shorter wavelengths (370 nm) is observed, followed by a dominant well-structured blue luminescence with maxima at 411, 435, 464.5, 498, and 537 nm with vibronic sidebands ca. 180 meV apart, close in energy to the CH2 bending vibration [56]. In addition, by monitoring the emission at 411 nm, the corresponding PLE spectrum in the studied spectral range exhibits excitation bands at 336 nm (with a shoulder at 320 nm), 262 nm, and 250 nm, well-matching the spectral region where π → π* and n → π* carbonyl groups absorbs [59], meaning that the blue emission can be populated via photon absorption in the excitation bands maxima. The shorter wavelength UV emission at 370 nm and the longer structured blue band are assigned, respectively, to the S1 → S0 fluorescence and T1 → S0 spin-forbidden phosphorescence transitions of α, β-unsaturated carbonyl species in the polymeric chain [54,57,58,60–65].

Optical characterization of the injected samples (HDPE and HDPE:CDs composites) was performed for each recycling cycle to evaluate their optical behavior when excited at 254 nm, which is the wavelength commonly used in commercial portable UV sources. Additionally, it is important to compare each sample with different CDs contents and each batch of samples at the same concentration.

This is an additional step to evaluate the degradation of the polymer after each recycling process, which involves a thermal mixing procedure and injection molding process. In fact, the thermal oxidation of HDPE considerably increases the absorption intensities of carbonyl, ester, and hydroxyl groups associated with the n-π* electronic transitions of the ethylenic group of the α, β-unsaturated carbonyl in thermally oxidized HDPE [65]. This behavior was expected, as the CDs also influence the perceived color of the injected samples, most likely caused by the presence of chromophoric groups [66]. Additionally, mechanical recycling is also responsible for other degradation reactions, such as photooxidation and mechanical shear oxidation [67]. Another problem associated with the mechanical recycling of polyethylene oxidation is the chain scission reactions and subsequent morphological changes caused by repeated heating of the sample [68,69]. This behavior is in good agreement with the results obtained from the crystallinity calculations, as it is also reduced for each recycling due to thermal oxidation. It is important to mention that this thermal oxidation leads to chain breakage, causing short chains and affecting the molecular structure and mechanical properties [70]. Fig. 4B displays the spectra comparing the samples from the neat polymer for all recycling cycles. A strong reduction in the blue phosphorescence was clearly observed after the first recycling cycle. This can be better analyzed from the data depicted in Fig. 4C, which shows the luminescence intensity (integrated area) for all the CDs concentrations and recycling cycles. The intensity was dramatically reduced after mechanical recycling. Fig. 4D displays the PL spectra acquired for the samples with 0.1 wt% CDs, revealing that the presence of CDs does not affect the spectral shape of the emission profile, which is expected because the CDs exhibited a very weak emission when excited at the same wavelength. An interesting behavior was observed for the sample with a 0.1 wt% CDs concentration (Fig. 4D), in which the blue phosphorescence intensity was gradually reduced for each recycling cycle, but not with such a pronounced intensity decrease, as in the case of the neat sample. It was observed that emission was influenced by the increase in CD content, which can be attributed to aggregation-caused quenching. This is the primary reason why the PL properties of CDs are not easily transferable from aqueous to the solid state [71]. For the undoped polymer, the blue phosphorescence intensity was reduced by approximately 90% in the first recycling cycle, which remained similar to that in the second cycle. Regarding the remaining samples (0.5 and 1 wt%), the behavior was different, with small differences between each recycling step (less than 2%) recorded.

This important discovery led us to explore the 0.1 wt% CDs concentration composites to a greater extent because the results showed an interesting potential for the creation of a separation method, which can determine which samples have been recycled and how many times they have been recycled. Thus, from this point on the focus of this study will be the composite with 0.1% CDs concentration to explore its potential as an optical tag for recycled HDPE samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the 3D printability and optical traceability of the HDPE nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: 3D printing, as a low-cost additive manufacturing technique, has many advantages over other production methods. It requires material in the form of a wire, which is deposited layer by layer to form the final product. It also allows for the introduction of a reinforcement material if needed. Once the polymeric composite is extruded into the wire, the design flexibility and input parameters of the 3D printer provide a large degree of freedom to produce printed specimens using in-house-prepared feedstock filaments. As a proof-of-concept, HDPE01CDs nanocomposites and neat polymer from three different recycling stages were 3D printed. For this purpose, a filament was extruded from each sample using the obtained polymer and nanocomposites (HDPE01CDs). The nanocomposite was selected based on its emission intensity and optical characteristics. Three versions of each material were printed: HDPE, HDPEV2, HDPEV3, HDPE01CDs, HDPE01CDsV2, and HDPE01CDsV3. Upon excitation with a handheld 254 nm UV source (Fig. 5 A), the color perceived for each sample allowed for a clear identification of each recycling cycle for the nanocomposite sample, whereas the neat polymer lost almost all emissions as it was mechanically recycled. No image manipulation is required; from the photograph, it is possible to identify each sample after computational processing using the program developed for that purpose. Additionally, it is important to highlight that the samples were printed under the same experimental conditions and printer specifications. This also demonstrates that the material retained its properties and was printable after enduring the previously detailed thermal processing. HDPE printed parts frequently suffer from warpage and crystallization-induced shrinkage owing to their poor printability; however, several studies have reported that when a filler is added to HDPE, the coefficient of thermal expansion decreases, reducing shrinkage during the cooling process and therefore controlling the warpage [72,73].

We developed a fast and expedite approach to verify the recycling cycles of HDPE:CDs based on the analyses of photographs collected with a smartphone (Fig. 5B). The advanced imaging capabilities, real-time feedback, software integration, and cost-effectiveness offered by these devices make them valuable tools in several application fields (environmental, biomedical, food, …) [74,75]. When samples containing CDs and subjected to different numbers of recycling cycles are illuminated with the UV excitation wavelength, they provide different brightness intensities. Thus, samples of the printed UA logo (approximately 35 × 38 mm2) obtained with different numbers of recycling cycles were illuminated with a 254 nm UV lamp, and a Galaxy A52s 5G (Samsung) smartphone was used to acquire five photographs of each sample (Fig. 5C). The test was carried out in a dark environment; therefore, there was no external interference with the brightness of the photographs.

Although applications are easily designed, allowing the analysis of the photographs and presentation of the results in a user-friendly manner, in this preliminary test, this treatment was performed afterward using only MATLAB code. Briefly, the photographs were uploaded and cropped according to an established analysis area (predefined by the user). Then, the intensity values for the three color channels, Red, Green, and Blue (RBG system), for the selected area of each photograph, as well as the average value obtained from the five photographs taken for each sample, were returned. The data in Fig. 5C, normalized using the results obtained for the neat HDPE sample, reveals that the intensity values obtained for the blue channel (with interest in this case) decrease with the number of recycling cycles of HDPE. These results prove that this practical and low-cost method can be easily used to distinguish recycled composites owing to the presence of CDs, which can function as an optical recycling marker.

In this study, the processing conditions for HDPE nanocomposites were optimized using a mixing and injection molding machine to produce homogeneous and reproducible specimens. Mechanical characterization revealed significant property enhancements, with the best-performing sample, HDPE05CDs, exhibiting a 7% increase in tensile strength and 9% increase in yield strength. XRD and DSC analyses confirmed that HDPE crystallinity remained largely unchanged across the CDs concentrations used in the nanocomposite formulations. Moreover, TGA demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs effectively delayed polymer degradation, resulting in improved thermal stability. Even after two reprocessing cycles, the HDPE nanocomposites displayed minimal degradation and retained their enhanced mechanical and thermal properties, underscoring the protective effect of the CDs against thermo-oxidative degradation. Additionally, the incorporation of 0.1 wt% CDs on the HDPE matrix achieved an impressive 98% reduction in the composite wear rate. The extended processing lifetime of the HDPE nanocomposites was further validated through additive manufacturing, where three consecutive recycling and 3D printing cycles were completed without significant performance deterioration.

The integration of CDs into the HDPE matrix was also explored for their potential use as optical tracers. Notably, the blue emission of the HDPE01CDs nanocomposite was partially quenched by UV irradiation at 254 nm. Additionally, the decrease in the emission intensity exhibited a strong correlation with the number of processing cycles. This observation enabled the development of a computational program capable of determining the number of recycling cycles of the HDPE01CDs samples using a standard smartphone camera. These findings are highly promising, paving the way for rapid, automated, and scalable HDPE recycling processes with significant potential to drive innovation in a circular economy strategy.
